corrections.description: Partition problems with inter-group constraints require algebraic setup—summing named parts to a known whole—not direct arithmetic on the total

details.fix: When a total is split into groups with relational constraints (equal, less than, more than), introduce a variable for the base group size and build a linear equation summing all groups to the total
details.our_error: The solver failed to decompose the group-splitting constraint into an algebraic equation with a shared unknown, returning the total instead of solving for the smallest partition
correct_steps: step 1: Identify three groups where two are equal size (call it x) and one is (x - 10)
step 2: Set up equation: x + x + (x - 10) = 200, i.e. 3x - 10 = 200
step 3: Solve: 3x = 210, x = 70
step 4: Smallest group = x - 10 = 70 - 10 = 60

corrections.description: Comparative phrases like 'N less than' define a variable relationship, not an independent subtraction operation; they must bind to the same unknown as the reference group

details.fix: Map 'is N less than [reference]' to a subtraction offset on a shared variable (x - N), not a standalone additive relation
details.our_error: The solver failed to decompose the group-splitting constraint into an algebraic equation with a shared unknown, returning the total instead of solving for the smallest partition
correct_steps: step 1: Identify three groups where two are equal size (call it x) and one is (x - 10)
step 2: Set up equation: x + x + (x - 10) = 200, i.e. 3x - 10 = 200
step 3: Solve: 3x = 210, x = 70
step 4: Smallest group = x - 10 = 70 - 10 = 60

corrections.description: Equality constraints between partitions act as multipliers on the unknown; missing them collapses the equation and loses the partition structure

details.fix: Extract the implicit constraint that two groups share the same size, producing a multiplier of 2 on the base variable in the sum equation
details.our_error: The solver failed to decompose the group-splitting constraint into an algebraic equation with a shared unknown, returning the total instead of solving for the smallest partition
correct_steps: step 1: Identify three groups where two are equal size (call it x) and one is (x - 10)
step 2: Set up equation: x + x + (x - 10) = 200, i.e. 3x - 10 = 200
step 3: Solve: 3x = 210, x = 70
step 4: Smallest group = x - 10 = 70 - 10 = 60

corrections.description: Comparison problems with parallel entities must resolve each entity's total independently before the final subtraction; collapsing to 1 entity merges the tracks

details.fix: comparison class requires: (1) compute total for entity A, (2) compute total for entity B, (3) subtract smaller from larger
details.our_error: The solver detected only 1 entity instead of 2 parallel entities, so it failed to compute separate totals for each coach and instead produced a single malformed calculation
correct_steps: step 1: identify baseball coach spending: 9 baseballs × $3 each = $27
step 2: identify basketball coach spending: 8 basketballs × $14 each = $112
step 3: compute difference: $112 - $27 = $85

corrections.description: When multiple actors each have their own quantity×rate pair, the entity splitter must create one entity per actor so each rate_chain resolves independently

details.fix: extract second entity (basketball coach) with quantity=8 and rate=14 dollars/each as a separate track
details.our_error: The solver detected only 1 entity instead of 2 parallel entities, so it failed to compute separate totals for each coach and instead produced a single malformed calculation
correct_steps: step 1: identify baseball coach spending: 9 baseballs × $3 each = $27
step 2: identify basketball coach spending: 8 basketballs × $14 each = $112
step 3: compute difference: $112 - $27 = $85

corrections.description: Time-shift phrases establish a temporal frame for the entire clause; all age references within that frame must be evaluated at the shifted time, not current time

details.key: two years ago ... half Jan's age
details.fix: When a time-shift phrase ('X years ago') scopes over a comparison, apply the time shift to ALL entities in that clause before computing relationships, then shift the result back to present
details.our_error: The solver applied 'half Jan's age' to Jan's current age (30) instead of Jan's age two years ago (28), and added 5 to get Mark's current age (35) without shifting the time reference back to present
correct_steps: step 1: Jan is 30 (given)
step 2: Two years ago, Jan was 30 - 2 = 28
step 3: Half of Jan's age two years ago: 28 / 2 = 14
step 4: Two years ago, Mark was 5 years older than that: 14 + 5 = 19
step 5: Mark's current age: 19 + 2 = 21
step 6: Jean is two years older than Mark: 21 + 2 = 23

corrections.description: Comparative age statements within a past-tense frame describe relationships at that past time; the result must be projected forward to get current age

details.key: was 5 years older than half Jan's age
details.fix: Compute: (Jan_age - time_shift) / 2 + 5 = Mark_age_at_shifted_time, then Mark_current = Mark_age_at_shifted_time + time_shift
details.our_error: The solver applied 'half Jan's age' to Jan's current age (30) instead of Jan's age two years ago (28), and added 5 to get Mark's current age (35) without shifting the time reference back to present
correct_steps: step 1: Jan is 30 (given)
step 2: Two years ago, Jan was 30 - 2 = 28
step 3: Half of Jan's age two years ago: 28 / 2 = 14
step 4: Two years ago, Mark was 5 years older than that: 14 + 5 = 19
step 5: Mark's current age: 19 + 2 = 21
step 6: Jean is two years older than Mark: 21 + 2 = 23

corrections.description: Temporal modifiers set the reference frame for all values in the clause; fractional operations on another entity's age must use that entity's age at the stated time, not their current age

details.key: two years ago ... half Jan's age
details.fix: When a time-shift modifier ('X years ago') scopes over a fractional reference ('half of Y's age'), apply the time shift to Y's age BEFORE computing the fraction, not after
details.our_error: The solver applied 'half Jan's age' to Jan's current age (30) instead of first shifting Jan's age back two years to match the 'two years ago' time frame, then halving
correct_steps: step 1: Jan is 30 (given)
step 2: Two years ago, Jan was 30 - 2 = 28
step 3: Half of Jan's age two years ago: 28 / 2 = 14
step 4: Two years ago, Mark was 5 years older than that: 14 + 5 = 19
step 5: Mark's current age: 19 + 2 = 21
step 6: Jean is two years older than Mark: 21 + 2 = 23

corrections.description: Temporal shifts ('two years ago') apply to the subject's age, not to every entity mentioned in the comparison. 'Half Jan's age' is a reference value, not a shifted value.

details.fix: When a fraction-of-age appears inside a temporal clause ('two years ago X was Y older than half Z's age'), compute the fraction on Z's CURRENT age, not Z's shifted age. The fraction refers to a static reference point unless explicitly modified by the temporal phrase.
details.our_error: The solver applied 'half Jan's age' to Jan's age two years ago (28/2=14) or misapplied the temporal shift, computing Mark_two_years_ago = half_jan_age + 5 using the wrong base, yielding Mark=35 instead of 22
correct_steps: step 1: Jan is 30 (given)
step 2: Half Jan's age = 30 / 2 = 15
step 3: Two years ago, Mark was 5 years older than half Jan's age → Mark_two_years_ago = 15 + 5 = 20
step 4: Mark's current age = 20 + 2 = 22 (undo the 'two years ago' shift)
step 5: Jean is two years older than Mark → Jean = 22 + 1 = 23... wait
step 5: Jean is two years older than Mark → Jean = 22 + 1 = 23

corrections.description: Past-tense age relations require solving in the past frame then adding the time offset to recover present age. Mixing the offset into the relational equation produces incorrect results.

details.fix: The temporal shift 'two years ago' must be inverted (+2) when solving forward from the past-tense equation to get current age. Ensure the +2 is applied to Mark's solved past age, not compounded into the comparison expression.
details.our_error: The solver applied 'half Jan's age' to Jan's age two years ago (28/2=14) or misapplied the temporal shift, computing Mark_two_years_ago = half_jan_age + 5 using the wrong base, yielding Mark=35 instead of 22
correct_steps: step 1: Jan is 30 (given)
step 2: Half Jan's age = 30 / 2 = 15
step 3: Two years ago, Mark was 5 years older than half Jan's age → Mark_two_years_ago = 15 + 5 = 20
step 4: Mark's current age = 20 + 2 = 22 (undo the 'two years ago' shift)
step 5: Jean is two years older than Mark → Jean = 22 + 1 = 23... wait
step 5: Jean is two years older than Mark → Jean = 22 + 1 = 23

corrections.description: Comparison problems require completing all steps for each entity before differencing; short-circuiting to a single entity's per-unit calculation skips both the quantity multiplication and the other entity's pipeline

details.fix: For multi-entity comparison problems, compute each entity's full pipeline (quantity × rate ± adjustments) independently, then subtract the two final totals
details.our_error: The solver computed each entity's total independently using only the percentage path for Sheila (30 × 0.9 = 27) without multiplying by quantity, and failed to compute Clara's total (quantity × rate - voucher) before taking the difference.
correct_steps: step 1: Clara's total before voucher = 20 pomegranates × $20/each = $400
step 2: Clara's final price = $400 - $2 voucher = $398
step 3: Sheila's total before discount = 20 pomegranates × $30/each = $600
step 4: Sheila's discount = 10% × $600 = $60
step 5: Sheila's final price = $600 - $60 = $540
step 6: Difference = $540 - $398 = $142

corrections.description: When text says 'discount on the total cost', the percentage operand is the aggregate, not the per-unit rate; the solver must resolve the multiplication before applying the percentage

details.fix: Percentage discount applies to (quantity × unit_price), not to unit_price alone; compute total first, then apply percentage
details.our_error: The solver computed each entity's total independently using only the percentage path for Sheila (30 × 0.9 = 27) without multiplying by quantity, and failed to compute Clara's total (quantity × rate - voucher) before taking the difference.
correct_steps: step 1: Clara's total before voucher = 20 pomegranates × $20/each = $400
step 2: Clara's final price = $400 - $2 voucher = $398
step 3: Sheila's total before discount = 20 pomegranates × $30/each = $600
step 4: Sheila's discount = 10% × $600 = $60
step 5: Sheila's final price = $600 - $60 = $540
step 6: Difference = $540 - $398 = $142

corrections.description: Flat adjustments (vouchers, fees, tips) that are not rates or percentages must be captured as additive offsets in the entity's pipeline; the solver dropped this value entirely

details.key: $2 off because she had a voucher
details.fix: Extract the $2 voucher as a flat subtraction applied after computing Clara's total (20 × $20 = $400 → $400 - $2 = $398)
details.our_error: The solver computed each entity's total independently using only the percentage path for Sheila (30 × 0.9 = 27) without multiplying by quantity, and failed to compute Clara's total (quantity × rate - voucher) before taking the difference.
correct_steps: step 1: Clara's total before voucher = 20 pomegranates × $20/each = $400
step 2: Clara's final price = $400 - $2 voucher = $398
step 3: Sheila's total before discount = 20 pomegranates × $30/each = $600
step 4: Sheila's discount = 10% × $600 = $60
step 5: Sheila's final price = $600 - $60 = $540
step 6: Difference = $540 - $398 = $142

corrections.description: Relational comparisons ('twice as much as', 'three times more than') imply a second entity whose derived quantity must be included in any aggregation over the group

details.fix: When a multiplicative_rel compares two entities ('twice as much as X'), both entities are consumers and must be summed for total demand
details.our_error: Failed to identify the girlfriend as a second consumer entity, so only computed vines for Steve's consumption (6*7/3=14... or 6*3=18 via wrong path) instead of total household consumption
correct_steps: step 1: Steve eats 6 tomatoes per day
step 2: His girlfriend eats half as much: 6 / 2 = 3 tomatoes per day
step 3: Total consumption: 6 + 3 = 9 tomatoes per day
step 4: Weekly consumption: 9 * 7 = 63 tomatoes per week
step 5: Vines needed: 63 / 3 = 21 vines

corrections.description: When multiplicative_rel establishes a relationship between entities, the rate_chain must operate on the aggregated total across all entities, not just the named one

details.fix: 1) extract named rate, 2) derive related entity rate via multiplier, 3) SUM all consumer rates, 4) unit-convert total rate to match supply rate period, 5) divide total demand by per-unit supply
details.our_error: Failed to identify the girlfriend as a second consumer entity, so only computed vines for Steve's consumption (6*7/3=14... or 6*3=18 via wrong path) instead of total household consumption
correct_steps: step 1: Steve eats 6 tomatoes per day
step 2: His girlfriend eats half as much: 6 / 2 = 3 tomatoes per day
step 3: Total consumption: 6 + 3 = 9 tomatoes per day
step 4: Weekly consumption: 9 * 7 = 63 tomatoes per week
step 5: Vines needed: 63 / 3 = 21 vines

corrections.description: Entity extraction must find all named subjects, not stop at 3; missing an entity causes wrong aggregation count and drops a term from the sum

details.fix: Extract all 4 named entities (Tony, Bobby, Kathy, Ricky) from the problem text
details.our_error: The solver detected only 3 entities instead of 4, and divided the base value by entity count instead of resolving each entity's rate through the relational chain and summing
correct_steps: step 1: Extract Tony's rate = 6 quarts/hour
step 2: Compute Bobby's rate = Tony - 1 = 6 - 1 = 5 quarts/hour
step 3: Compute Kathy's rate = 2 × Bobby = 2 × 5 = 10 quarts/hour
step 4: Compute Ricky's rate = Kathy - 2 = 10 - 2 = 8 quarts/hour
step 5: Sum all rates = 6 + 5 + 10 + 8 = 29 quarts/hour

corrections.description: Rate chains require sequential resolution where each entity depends on a prior entity's resolved value; dividing a base by count is wrong when entities have different derived rates

details.fix: For rate_chain problems: resolve each entity's value by walking the dependency chain (base → derived → derived), then sum all resolved values
details.our_error: The solver detected only 3 entities instead of 4, and divided the base value by entity count instead of resolving each entity's rate through the relational chain and summing
correct_steps: step 1: Extract Tony's rate = 6 quarts/hour
step 2: Compute Bobby's rate = Tony - 1 = 6 - 1 = 5 quarts/hour
step 3: Compute Kathy's rate = 2 × Bobby = 2 × 5 = 10 quarts/hour
step 4: Compute Ricky's rate = Kathy - 2 = 10 - 2 = 8 quarts/hour
step 5: Sum all rates = 6 + 5 + 10 + 8 = 29 quarts/hour

corrections.description: Division by entity count assumes equal distribution; relational chains (less than, twice as many, fewer than) define distinct per-entity values that must be individually computed and then aggregated

details.fix: Do not divide the base rate by entity count; instead compute each entity's rate independently via relational operators (minus, times) then sum
details.our_error: The solver detected only 3 entities instead of 4, and divided the base value by entity count instead of resolving each entity's rate through the relational chain and summing
correct_steps: step 1: Extract Tony's rate = 6 quarts/hour
step 2: Compute Bobby's rate = Tony - 1 = 6 - 1 = 5 quarts/hour
step 3: Compute Kathy's rate = 2 × Bobby = 2 × 5 = 10 quarts/hour
step 4: Compute Ricky's rate = Kathy - 2 = 10 - 2 = 8 quarts/hour
step 5: Sum all rates = 6 + 5 + 10 + 8 = 29 quarts/hour

corrections.description: Any problem with chained fractional consumption (A eats fraction, then B eats fraction of remainder) requires one inversion node per consumer; collapsing them loses intermediate quantities

details.fix: Each 'fraction of remainder' phrase must create a new node in the chain; working_backwards must invert each step sequentially from the known remainder back to the original, not collapse multiple remainder steps
details.our_error: The solver built only 1 node instead of chaining two working_backwards steps, collapsing the two sequential 'half of remainder' operations into a single pass that returned the final remainder (270) as the answer
correct_steps: step 1: Allosaurus ate the last 270 kg, which is what remained after velociraptors scavenged
step 2: Velociraptors ate half of what was left, so 270 kg is the other half → before velociraptors: 270 * 2 = 540 kg
step 3: T-Rex ate half the triceratops, so 540 kg is the other half → before T-Rex: 540 * 2 = 1080 kg

corrections.description: Working backwards through a fractional consumption requires dividing by the remaining fraction (or equivalently multiplying by its reciprocal), not treating the known value as the answer

details.fix: When working backwards from a remainder after someone took half, multiply by 2 (invert the fraction), not pass through unchanged; each 'ate half' leaving a remainder means remainder = original * 0.5, so original = remainder / 0.5 = remainder * 2
details.our_error: The solver built only 1 node instead of chaining two working_backwards steps, collapsing the two sequential 'half of remainder' operations into a single pass that returned the final remainder (270) as the answer
correct_steps: step 1: Allosaurus ate the last 270 kg, which is what remained after velociraptors scavenged
step 2: Velociraptors ate half of what was left, so 270 kg is the other half → before velociraptors: 270 * 2 = 540 kg
step 3: T-Rex ate half the triceratops, so 540 kg is the other half → before T-Rex: 540 * 2 = 1080 kg
